How Much Are You Willing to Sacrifice for What You Want?
We all have to make choices. HexSchool's CEO Liao once shared a video —
— and at one point he asks: how much are you willing to sacrifice to hit your goal?
Something happened recently that got me thinking about this, so I want to share some of my own experience.

When I started learning to code, I went the self-taught route. There weren't as many online courses back then — I had to rely on Googling and buying books. As you'd expect, the learning didn't go great, because books teach you off a rigid syllabus that often diverges from real-world dev environments.

I hit a lot of roadblocks in that learning process. I didn't grow up coding, so I had zero baseline programming intuition (and not great memory either). To actually grasp the basics, I had to commit to giving things up. What I gave up was my college social life — most people in college go on trips with classmates, do orientation activities together, all that. I was sitting at a computer trying to figure out how programs actually worked. The trade-off: I lost the social/fun side of college, but I picked up programming foundations, project management, and client communication (the last two because I was a project manager during school).

In the middle of learning to code, you naturally hit real-life pressure. I stepped away from coding for about six months to a year and did sales work instead. The reason: I realized I wasn't articulate enough with customers, my real-time reactions were slow. So I went into credit card sales, real estate sales, food sales — anything to train myself to stay calm in front of customers and not get rattled. During school I'd also volunteered to be the presenter in group projects to build up presentation poise (good prep beforehand is critical).
Learning new tech, it's pretty easy to hit setbacks. I had moments where I almost gave up. The main reason was that I'd picked the wrong path.
Earlier I mentioned I'd sacrificed things to learn the basics. The language I'd been investing in was PHP & MySQL — backend. Not that the language is bad.
So why did I almost give up? Because I realized I didn't know what to actually

with it. Learning the language wasn't making me more valuable or opening up more work opportunities, so I started leaning toward quitting. I stopped writing PHP & MySQL for at least six months and tried C, C++, C#, Java, Python — looking for whatever rekindled my original motivation to code.

Eventually, through a series of coincidences, a manager at my company asked me to do frontend, and that's when I realized the reason I'd started coding was actually frontend. So I'd spent 3–4 years before finding the area I actually liked.
What I sacrificed to switch to frontend

It's been around 8–9 months since I committed to frontend. The thought that's kept me going is: "I finally found something I'm genuinely interested in — this time I'm going to make it work." That thought still sustains me. Of course, this round had its own sacrifices: leisure time, health, and family time.
I'd sit at the computer for 8–12 hours straight just to nail down frontend fundamentals and build some small projects. My main learning source was Udemy, so I track learning minutes per month.
The peak month was around December 2018, hitting 2000+ minutes.
Basically every month was 1000+ minutes — actually more than that, since I'd pause videos to take notes and dig into the underlying principles.
A lot of the time we have to choose. In economics this is called opportunity cost.
What's opportunity cost? In short: when you pick option A, you have to give up options B, C, D — but you also get the option that has the highest value to you.

If you're still unclear, ask yourself: how much are you willing to sacrifice to learn this thing? In my case I gave up leisure, health, and family time in exchange for frontend fundamentals. What did I get out of it, results-wise? On one hand, when I hit a wall I try hard to break through. On the other hand, frontend dev makes me genuinely happy — to the point where, once my frontend gets stable, I want to challenge backend dev again.

We all have to make choices. HexSchool's CEO Liao once shared a video on the full frontend job-hunt playbook, and at one point he talks about how much you're willing to sacrifice to reach your goals. Lately I've been thinking about that question with my own life, so here's a personal post.
